items: Submit a change request with scope and risk assessment
Obtain approval from the Change Advisory Board
Test the change in a lab or staging environment
Implement the approved change during the maintenance window
Document the results and update configuration records
explanation: Formal change management flows: submit the change request with scope/risk, test it in a lab or staging environment, obtain Change Advisory Board approval, implement during the maintenance window, then document the results and update configuration records.

question: A technician is performing bottom-up OSI model troubleshooting. Arrange the layers in the order they should be checked, starting from the lowest layer.
items: Network Layer (Layer 3)
Physical Layer (Layer 1)
Data Link Layer (Layer 2)
Transport Layer (Layer 4)
Application Layer (Layer 7)
explanation: Bottom-up OSI troubleshooting starts at Layer 1 (Physical) and works upward: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, then Application - verifying each layer before moving higher.

question: Evaluate each statement about DNS and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: A CNAME record can point to another CNAME record (CNAME chaining).
An MX record must always contain the direct IP address of the mail server.
TTL values in DNS responses control how long resolvers cache the record.
A domain can have multiple MX records, each with a different priority value.
explanation: True: CNAME chaining is permitted (though discouraged), TTL controls how long resolvers cache a record, and a domain can have multiple MX records with different priorities. False: an MX record points to a hostname, not a direct IP address.

question: Evaluate each statement about Power over Ethernet (PoE) standards and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: IEEE 802.3af delivers a maximum of 15.4 W per port at the power sourcing equipment.
IEEE 802.3at (PoE+) can deliver up to 60 W per port.
PoE can supply power to IP cameras over standard Cat5e or Cat6 cabling.
A PoE injector allows a non-PoE switch port to power PoE-capable devices.
explanation: True: 802.3af supplies up to 15.4 W at the PSE, PoE runs over standard Cat5e/Cat6, and an injector adds PoE to a non-PoE port. False: 802.3at (PoE+) delivers about 30 W, not 60 W (60/100 W is 802.3bt).

question: Evaluate each statement about IEEE 802.1X port-based authentication and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: The supplicant is the end device (e.g., laptop or IP phone) requesting network access.
In 802.1X, the authentication server is typically the network switch or wireless AP.
EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) is used to carry credentials between the supplicant and the authenticator.
Without 802.1X enforcement, any device physically connected to a switch port may gain network access.
explanation: True: the supplicant is the requesting end device, EAP carries credentials between supplicant and authenticator, and without 802.1X any connected device may gain access. False: the authentication server is a RADIUS server, not the switch/AP (that is the authenticator).

question: A workstation is unable to reach the DHCP server and automatically configures itself with a 169.254.x.x address. Which term describes this self-assigned addressing mechanism?
explanation: Automatic Private IP Addressing (APIPA) self-assigns a 169.254.0.0/16 address when no DHCP server responds, enabling only local-subnet communication. SLAAC/DHCPv6 are IPv6 mechanisms and static assignment is manual.

question: A content delivery network (CDN) routes each client's request to the nearest edge server without the client knowing which physical server will respond. Which addressing type is this?
explanation: Anycast advertises one address from multiple locations; routing delivers each client to the topologically nearest node, which is how CDNs steer requests to the closest edge. Unicast is one-to-one, broadcast one-to-all on a subnet, multicast one-to-group.

question: A host on 192.168.10.0/24 needs to communicate with a server on 10.0.0.0/8. What must be configured on the host for it to reach the remote network?
explanation: To reach a network outside its own subnet, a host must have a default gateway (router) configured to forward off-subnet traffic. Static ARP, secondary IPs, and loopback interfaces do not provide inter-network routing.

question: An administrator summarizes a group of networks using CIDR notation /22. How many total IP addresses (including network and broadcast) are represented by a single /22 block?
explanation: A /22 has 10 host bits, so it contains 2^10 = 1024 total addresses (1022 usable after the network and broadcast). It is equivalent to four contiguous /24s.

question: A developer runs a local web server and accesses it via http://127.0.0.1. Which address type is 127.0.0.1?
explanation: 127.0.0.1 is the IPv4 loopback address (the 127.0.0.0/8 block) used to reach services on the local host itself. It is not APIPA, multicast, or an RFC 1918 private address.

question: The private IPv4 address range defined by RFC 1918 for the 172.x.x.x space spans from 172.16.0.0 through which ending address?
explanation: RFC 1918 defines the private range 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255 (172.16.0.0/12). 172.15.x and 172.32.x fall outside the private block.

question: IPv6 link-local addresses are automatically configured on every IPv6-enabled interface and are used for on-link communication. Which prefix identifies IPv6 link-local addresses?
explanation: IPv6 link-local addresses use the fe80::/10 prefix and are auto-configured on every IPv6 interface for on-link communication. 2001::/16 is global unicast space, fc00::/7 is unique local, and ff00::/8 is multicast.

question: Which OSI layer is responsible for end-to-end communication, flow control, and segmentation of data into segments, and includes protocols such as TCP and UDP?
explanation: Layer 4 (Transport) provides end-to-end delivery, segmentation into segments, flow control, and multiplexing via ports; TCP and UDP operate here. Data Link, Network, and Session are layers 2, 3, and 5.

question: SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) is used to set up, manage, and tear down VoIP calls. At which OSI layer does SIP primarily operate?
explanation: SIP is an application-layer (Layer 7) signaling protocol that establishes, modifies, and terminates VoIP sessions; the media itself is carried separately by RTP.

question: Which protocol is specifically designed to transport real-time audio and video streams over IP networks and provides timing, sequencing, and payload identification?
explanation: RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol) carries real-time audio/video with sequence numbers, timestamps, and payload type identification for jitter buffering and sync. SIP/H.323 handle signaling and RTSP controls streaming sessions.

question: A router running OSPF learns a route to 10.10.0.0/16. What is the default administrative distance for OSPF-learned routes?
explanation: OSPF's default administrative distance is 110. EIGRP internal is 90, RIP is 120, and EIGRP external is 170 - lower AD is preferred when multiple protocols offer the same route.

question: An engineer is comparing routing protocol administrative distances. Which value represents the default administrative distance for EIGRP internal routes?
explanation: EIGRP internal routes have a default administrative distance of 90, making them preferred over OSPF (110) and RIP (120) for the same prefix.

question: WPA3-Personal replaces the PSK handshake used in WPA2-Personal with a more secure key exchange method that protects against offline dictionary attacks. What is this method called?
explanation: WPA3-Personal replaces the WPA2 PSK 4-way handshake with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, a dragonfly key exchange) which resists offline dictionary attacks and provides forward secrecy. PEAP/EAP-TLS are enterprise methods and TKIP is legacy WPA encryption.

question: Which feature introduced in 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) allows an access point to simultaneously transmit data to multiple clients in different frequency sub-bands, improving efficiency in dense environments?
explanation: OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiple Access) in 802.11ax subdivides a channel into resource units so an AP can serve multiple clients in one transmission, greatly improving efficiency in dense environments. MIMO/beamforming/band steering address different problems.

question: A wireless access point supports MU-MIMO. What is the primary benefit of Multi-User MIMO compared to Single-User MIMO?
choices: Increases range by focusing signal toward the client
Allows simultaneous data transmission to multiple clients
Doubles the maximum bandwidth of each individual client
Eliminates the need for 5 GHz band operation
explanation: MU-MIMO lets the AP transmit to several clients at the same time using separate spatial streams, increasing aggregate throughput. SU-MIMO serves one client at a time. It does not itself extend range or remove the need for 5 GHz.

question: A network engineer is advising on deploying a wireless network in an office building with many neighboring networks. Which frequency band provides more non-overlapping channels and less interference from neighboring networks?
explanation: The 5 GHz band offers many more non-overlapping channels than 2.4 GHz (which has only 3), reducing co-channel interference in dense, neighbor-heavy environments. 900 MHz is not used for Wi-Fi and 6 GHz requires Wi-Fi 6E hardware.

question: An administrator hides the SSID on all access points hoping to improve wireless security. What is the actual security value of SSID hiding?
choices: Minimal — the SSID can still be discovered by passive sniffing of probe responses
Significant — clients cannot connect without manually entering the SSID
High — SSID is encrypted and cannot be intercepted
Complete — unauthorized devices cannot detect the network exists
explanation: SSID hiding provides minimal security: the SSID is still transmitted in association/probe frames and is trivially recovered by passive sniffing. It is security by obscurity, not encryption or true concealment.

question: A company deploys multiple access points throughout a building, all broadcasting the same SSID. Clients roam between APs seamlessly. What wireless architecture is this?
explanation: Multiple APs sharing one SSID so clients roam seamlessly form an Extended Service Set (ESS). A single AP cell is a BSS, ad hoc is IBSS, and a mesh uses wireless backhaul between APs.

question: The 802.11 wireless medium access method is CSMA/CA. What is the primary reason wireless uses Collision Avoidance instead of the Collision Detection used by wired Ethernet?
choices: Wireless uses full-duplex transmission, so collisions cannot occur
Wireless NICs cannot detect collisions while transmitting because the transmitted signal overwhelms any received signal
Wireless uses token passing, which eliminates the need for collision detection
Wireless operates at Layer 1 only and does not require media access control
explanation: A wireless NIC cannot hear collisions while transmitting because its own outgoing signal overwhelms any received signal, so 802.11 uses CSMA/CA (avoidance with RTS/CTS and ACKs) rather than the CSMA/CD used by legacy wired Ethernet.

question: A hospital is deploying powered medical devices requiring up to 71 W over PoE. Which IEEE standard is capable of delivering this power level?
explanation: 802.3bt Type 4 delivers up to about 71-90 W at the PSE, enough for high-draw devices. 802.3af (~15 W), 802.3at (~30 W) fall short, and 802.3bz is a multi-gig speed standard, not PoE.

question: During a building renovation, a network technician must run cable through a ceiling space used as a return air plenum for the HVAC system. Which type of cable is required by fire code?
explanation: Cable in an air-handling (plenum) space must be plenum-rated (CMP), which uses low-smoke, flame-retardant jacketing required by fire code. Riser (CMR) and general-purpose (CM) are not permitted in plenum spaces.

question: A technician is punching down Cat6 cable to an RJ-45 keystone jack using T568B wiring. Which color goes on pin 1 (pair 3, tip) according to T568B?
explanation: Under T568B, pin 1 is White/Orange and pin 2 is Orange (pair 2). White/Green is pin 3 in T568B, White/Blue is pin 5, and White/Brown is pin 7.

question: A company's disaster recovery plan states that the organization cannot lose more than 4 hours of transaction data after a failure. Which recovery metric defines this maximum acceptable data loss?
explanation: Recovery Point Objective (RPO) defines the maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time (here, 4 hours), driving backup/replication frequency. RTO is downtime tolerance; MTTR/MTBF are reliability metrics.

question: After a major network failure, management asks how long the IT team has to restore service before the business impact becomes unacceptable. Which disaster recovery metric defines this maximum tolerable downtime?
explanation: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) is the maximum tolerable time to restore service after an outage before business impact is unacceptable. RPO is data-loss tolerance, while MTBF/MTTR describe failure and repair averages.

question: An organization needs a disaster recovery site where all infrastructure is preconfigured, data is replicated in real time, and failover can occur within minutes. Which DR site type meets this requirement?
explanation: A hot site has fully provisioned, running infrastructure with real-time data replication, enabling failover in minutes. Warm sites need some setup/restore, and cold sites are just space and power.

question: A network diagram shows routers labeled with IP addresses, subnet masks, and routing protocol areas, but does not show physical cable paths or equipment locations. Which type of diagram is this?
explanation: A logical diagram shows IP addressing, subnets, VLANs, and routing relationships rather than physical cable paths or equipment placement (which are physical/rack/floor-plan diagrams).

question: An SNMPv2c community string is configured on a network switch. What function does the community string serve?
choices: It encrypts SNMP traffic between manager and agent
It acts as a shared password controlling read or write access
It specifies the OID tree the manager can query
It identifies the SNMP version used for trap messages
explanation: An SNMPv1/v2c community string is effectively a shared, cleartext password that grants read-only or read-write access to the agent. It does not encrypt traffic or scope the OID tree; SNMPv3 adds real security.

question: Network devices are configured to forward syslog messages to a central log server. Which UDP port does syslog use by default?
explanation: Syslog uses UDP port 514 by default for forwarding messages to a collector. UDP 161/162 are SNMP get/trap, and UDP 123 is NTP.

question: An organization activates a disaster recovery site that has the necessary building and power infrastructure, but requires equipment to be procured and data to be restored before it can be used. Which DR site type is this?
explanation: A cold site provides only the facility, power, and cooling; equipment must be procured and data restored before it is usable, making it the cheapest but slowest DR option. Warm/hot sites are progressively more ready.

question: A network administrator stores configuration backup files only on the same devices being backed up. After a device failure, the backups are also lost. Which best practice was violated?
choices: Off-site or geographically separate backup storage
explanation: Storing backups only on the device being backed up means a device failure destroys the backups too; best practice requires off-site or geographically separate backup storage (e.g., the 3-2-1 rule).

question: An IPSec VPN tunnel is configured using ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload). Which security services does ESP provide?
choices: Integrity only — no encryption or authentication
explanation: IPSec ESP provides confidentiality (encryption) plus integrity and authentication of the payload. AH provides integrity/authentication only, and key exchange is handled separately by IKE.

question: An IPSec implementation uses AH (Authentication Header). What does AH provide that distinguishes it from ESP?
choices: Data integrity and authentication without encryption
explanation: IPSec AH (Authentication Header) provides data integrity, origin authentication, and replay protection but NO encryption (no confidentiality). ESP can provide encryption plus integrity, which is why ESP is far more commonly deployed.

question: A security team is upgrading remote access VPN from IKEv1 to IKEv2. Which improvement does IKEv2 offer over IKEv1?
choices: Built-in MOBIKE support for mobile clients and faster initial negotiation
Elimination of the need for pre-shared keys
Support for GRE tunneling without additional configuration
explanation: IKEv2 adds native MOBIKE (mobility/multihoming for roaming clients), a leaner four-message exchange for faster setup, built-in NAT traversal, and dead-peer detection. It is not SSL/TLS compatible, still supports PSKs, and does not auto-configure GRE.

question: Remote workers need VPN access without installing a dedicated VPN client. They should be able to connect from any HTTPS-capable browser through firewalls that block IPSec. Which VPN type is best suited?
explanation: A clientless SSL/TLS VPN runs over HTTPS (TCP 443) through any browser, so no dedicated client is needed and it passes firewalls that block IPSec/ESP. IPSec, GRE, and L2TP/IPSec all require client software and specific protocol/port access.

question: An attacker calls an employee, claims to be from the IT helpdesk, and convinces them to reveal their network password. Which social engineering technique is this?
explanation: Voice-based social engineering over a phone call is vishing. Phishing is email-based, smishing uses SMS, and spear phishing is a targeted email attack - none of which describe a phone call.

question: An attacker on a LAN sends forged ARP replies associating their MAC address with the default gateway's IP. What attack is being performed, and what is the goal?
choices: MAC flooding — to overflow the switch's CAM table
DNS poisoning — to redirect web traffic to a fake server
Replay attack — to resend captured authentication packets
ARP spoofing — to intercept traffic destined for the gateway (MITM)
explanation: Sending forged ARP replies binding the attacker's MAC to the gateway's IP is ARP spoofing/poisoning; victims send gateway-bound traffic to the attacker, enabling a man-in-the-middle. MAC flooding, DNS poisoning, and replay attacks work differently.

question: An organization is implementing a zero-trust security architecture. Which foundational principle defines zero trust?
choices: Never trust, always verify — every access request must be authenticated and authorized regardless of network location
Trust internal network traffic implicitly; verify only external connections
Block all incoming traffic by default; allow only traffic matching explicit rules
Segment the network into zones and trust traffic within the same zone
explanation: Zero trust is built on 'never trust, always verify': every request is authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the network. The other options describe perimeter or segmentation models, not zero trust.

question: A laptop connected to wireless is experiencing high packet loss and retransmissions, but the signal strength appears adequate. Which condition is most likely causing this?
choices: Low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) due to RF interference
explanation: Adequate signal strength with high loss and retransmissions points to a poor signal-to-noise ratio from RF interference (noise floor too high), not an addressing or DHCP problem.

question: A network switch shows CPU utilization spiking to 100% and all connected devices lose connectivity simultaneously. A traffic capture shows millions of broadcast frames per second. What is the most likely cause?
choices: Rogue DHCP server on the network
Broadcast storm caused by a switching loop
Spanning tree port stuck in forwarding state
explanation: Millions of broadcasts per second with 100% switch CPU and total connectivity loss is a classic broadcast storm caused by a Layer 2 switching loop with no/failed STP. A rogue DHCP server or CAM overflow would not generate that broadcast volume.

question: A technician runs 'show interfaces' on a switch and notices a high count of runt frames. What does a high runt count indicate?
choices: Frames exceeding the MTU of 1518 bytes
Frames smaller than the minimum 64-byte Ethernet frame size
explanation: Runts are frames smaller than the 64-byte Ethernet minimum, usually caused by collisions or a duplex mismatch. Oversized frames are giants, and CRC errors are counted separately as FCS errors.

question: Which TWO features were introduced in 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) that were NOT present in 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)? (Select 2)
choices: BSS Coloring to reduce inter-BSS interference
explanation: OFDMA and BSS Coloring are new in 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6). MU-MIMO (downlink) and 256-QAM were already introduced in 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5).

question: When IPSec is deployed in tunnel mode rather than transport mode, which TWO statements are true? (Select 2)
choices: Only the payload is encrypted; the original IP header remains intact
The entire original IP packet (header + payload) is encapsulated
A new outer IP header is added to route the encrypted packet
Transport mode and tunnel mode provide identical protection
explanation: In tunnel mode the entire original IP packet (header + payload) is encapsulated and a new outer IP header is added for routing between gateways. Transport mode protects only the payload and keeps the original header, so the modes are not equivalent.

question: A wireless technician is investigating intermittent connectivity issues in a 2.4 GHz network. Which TWO sources are common causes of RF interference on the 2.4 GHz band? (Select 2)
choices: Overlapping channels from neighboring 2.4 GHz networks
Ethernet switches on the same VLAN
explanation: The 2.4 GHz band is congested by microwave ovens (and Bluetooth/cordless phones) and by overlapping channels from neighboring 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi. 5 GHz APs and wired switches do not interfere with 2.4 GHz RF.

question: Match each IPv4 address class to its correct address range.
explanation: Classful ranges: Class A 1-126, Class B 128-191, Class C 192-223, and Class D 224-239 (multicast). The 127.x block is reserved for loopback between A and B.

question: Match each VPN or tunneling protocol to its primary characteristic.
itemsRight: Provides data integrity and authentication without encryption
Encapsulates any Layer 3 protocol; provides no built-in encryption
Provides both encryption and authentication for the payload
Operates at the application layer and traverses firewalls via HTTPS (port 443)
explanation: ESP encrypts and authenticates the payload, AH authenticates without encryption, an SSL/TLS VPN runs at the application layer over HTTPS/443 to traverse firewalls, and GRE encapsulates any Layer 3 protocol but adds no encryption.

question: Arrange the following steps in the correct sequence for a formal network change management process.
items: Submit a change request documenting scope, risk, and rollback plan
Obtain approval from the Change Advisory Board (CAB)
Test and validate the change in a staging or lab environment
Implement the change during the approved maintenance window
Document outcomes and update configuration records
explanation: Change management order: submit the request (scope, risk, rollback), test/validate in staging, obtain CAB approval, implement in the maintenance window, then document outcomes and update configuration records.

question: A user reports intermittent wireless drops. Arrange the following troubleshooting actions in the most logical order.
items: Check client signal strength, SNR, and observed data rates
Confirm the client is associated to the correct SSID with valid credentials
Use a spectrum analyzer or Wi-Fi scanner to identify RF interference sources
Reboot the AP and test client connectivity after reconnection
Escalate to the wireless vendor or perform a full site survey if issue persists
explanation: Logical wireless troubleshooting: confirm correct SSID/credentials and association, check signal strength/SNR/data rates, scan for RF interference with a spectrum analyzer, reboot/retest the AP, then escalate or run a full site survey if unresolved.

question: Evaluate each statement about disaster recovery (DR) site types and metrics, and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: A hot site maintains real-time or near-real-time data replication and can fail over within minutes.
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines the maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time.
A cold site has all production servers pre-installed, configured, and ready to power on immediately.
RTO (Recovery Time Objective) defines the maximum tolerable time to restore normal operations after a failure.
explanation: True: a hot site replicates data in near-real-time and fails over in minutes, RPO is acceptable data loss in time, and RTO is the tolerable time to restore service. False: a cold site has only space/power - servers are not pre-installed and ready.

question: Evaluate each statement about IPSec and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: IPSec AH (Authentication Header) provides data confidentiality through encryption.
IPSec ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) can provide both encryption and data integrity.
IPSec can operate in either transport mode or tunnel mode depending on the deployment.
IKE (Internet Key Exchange) uses TCP port 500 for key negotiation.
explanation: True: ESP can provide both encryption and integrity, and IPSec runs in transport or tunnel mode. False: AH provides no confidentiality (no encryption), and IKE uses UDP port 500 (not TCP).

question: Evaluate each statement about IPv6 and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses, providing approximately 3.4 × 10³⁸ possible addresses.
IPv6 networks require NAT because the global address space is insufficient for all devices.
IPv6 uses NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) to perform functions equivalent to ARP in IPv4.
IPv6 link-local addresses (fe80::/10) are routable across the public internet.
explanation: True: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses (~3.4 x 10^38) and NDP replaces ARP. False: the huge address space removes the need for NAT, and fe80::/10 link-local addresses are never routed off-link or across the internet.

question: A legacy system requires unencrypted remote terminal access. Which protocol provides this, and on which TCP port does it listen?
explanation: Telnet provides unencrypted remote terminal access on TCP port 23. SSH (TCP 22) is the encrypted alternative; Telnet sends credentials and data in cleartext.

question: An administrator needs encrypted remote access to a Linux server. Which protocol and port provides encrypted terminal sessions?
explanation: SSH provides encrypted remote terminal sessions on TCP port 22. Telnet (23) is cleartext, RDP (3389) is graphical remote desktop, and SFTP runs over SSH, not port 21.

question: A web server must accept encrypted browser connections. Which port must be open on the firewall for HTTPS traffic?
explanation: HTTPS uses TCP port 443 for TLS-encrypted web traffic. Port 80 is plain HTTP, and 8080/8443 are common non-standard alternates, not the firewall port required for standard HTTPS.

question: A Windows administrator needs to remotely control a desktop using the Remote Desktop Protocol. Which TCP port does RDP use by default?
explanation: Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) listens on TCP port 3389 by default. Ports 22, 23, and 443 are SSH, Telnet, and HTTPS respectively.

question: An application needs to query an Active Directory server for user authentication over the standard unencrypted LDAP port. Which port should be used?
explanation: Standard (unencrypted) LDAP uses TCP/UDP port 389. LDAPS (LDAP over TLS) uses 636, Kerberos uses 88, and 443 is HTTPS. [Answer key corrected: 389.]

question: A security administrator requires all LDAP queries to be encrypted using TLS. Which port is used for LDAPS (LDAP over TLS)?
explanation: LDAPS (LDAP over TLS/SSL) uses TCP port 636. Port 389 is plaintext LDAP, 443 is HTTPS, and 88 is Kerberos.

question: Kerberos is the default authentication protocol in Windows Active Directory environments. Which port does Kerberos use?
explanation: Kerberos authentication uses port 88 in Active Directory environments. 389/636 are LDAP/LDAPS and 464 is the Kerberos password-change service (kpasswd), not primary authentication.

question: Network devices must synchronize their clocks with an NTP server. On which UDP port does NTP operate?
explanation: NTP synchronizes clocks over UDP port 123. UDP 161/162 are SNMP, and 514 is syslog.

question: OSPF is a link-state routing protocol. How does a link-state routing protocol differ from a distance-vector protocol like RIP?
choices: Link-state routers share their full routing table with neighbors periodically
Distance-vector protocols use SPF (Dijkstra) to calculate best paths
Link-state routers build a complete map of the network topology and run SPF to calculate best paths
Distance-vector protocols exchange LSAs to maintain a topological database
explanation: Link-state protocols (OSPF) flood LSAs so every router builds a complete topology database, then each runs the Dijkstra SPF algorithm to compute best paths. Distance-vector protocols (RIP) only share their routing table with directly connected neighbors.

question: RIP (Routing Information Protocol) is limited to small networks. What is the maximum hop count allowed by RIP before a route is considered unreachable?
explanation: RIP treats 16 hops as infinity (unreachable), so the maximum usable hop count is 15. This limits RIP to small networks.

question: In an OSPF multi-area deployment, all areas must connect to a central area that acts as the transit backbone. What is this backbone area called?
explanation: In multi-area OSPF, Area 0 is the backbone; all other areas must connect to it (directly or via a virtual link) so inter-area routing transits the backbone. Stub areas are a type of non-backbone area.

question: An administrator needs to isolate voice traffic from data traffic on the same physical switches without running separate cabling. Which technology achieves this logical separation at Layer 2?
explanation: VLANs provide logical Layer 2 segmentation on shared switches/cabling, isolating voice from data into separate broadcast domains. QoS marks priority, subnetting is Layer 3, and NAT translates addresses - none of which segment Layer 2.

question: A switch port is configured as a trunk to carry traffic for VLANs 10, 20, and 30 between switches. Which protocol adds a 4-byte tag to Ethernet frames to identify which VLAN a frame belongs to?
explanation: IEEE 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte VLAN tag (TPID + TCI with the 12-bit VLAN ID) into Ethernet frames on trunk links. 802.1D is STP, ISL is legacy Cisco-proprietary tagging, and 802.3ad is link aggregation.

question: A security engineer notices that untagged frames arriving on an 802.1Q trunk link are being forwarded into a sensitive VLAN. Which trunk configuration item determines which VLAN receives untagged traffic?
explanation: The native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk carries untagged frames; if it is misconfigured or left at the default, untagged traffic can land in an unintended VLAN (VLAN hopping risk). Access/management/voice VLANs serve other roles.

question: During STP convergence, all switches participate in a root bridge election. How is the root bridge determined?
choices: The switch with the highest MAC address
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC address)
The switch with the most ports
The switch configured as a Layer 3 switch
explanation: The STP root bridge is the switch with the lowest Bridge ID (bridge priority followed by MAC address as tiebreaker). Port count and Layer 3 capability are irrelevant to the election.

question: How many port states does classic IEEE 802.1D STP define?
explanation: Classic 802.1D STP defines five port states: disabled, blocking, listening, learning, and forwarding. RSTP later collapsed these into discarding, learning, and forwarding.

question: A network engineer replaces STP with RSTP (IEEE 802.1w) on all switches. What is the primary advantage of RSTP over classic STP?
choices: RSTP converges significantly faster (seconds vs. 30–50 seconds for STP)
RSTP eliminates the need for BPDUs
RSTP uses a distributed algorithm instead of a root bridge
explanation: RSTP (802.1w) converges in a few seconds versus 30-50 seconds for classic STP by using proposal/agreement handshakes and port roles. It still uses BPDUs and a root bridge and does not change the 4094 VLAN limit.

question: Four Gigabit Ethernet links between two core switches are combined using EtherChannel. What are the two primary benefits of EtherChannel?
choices: Eliminates the need for STP and provides 10 Gbps per link
Increases the MTU and reduces latency per frame
Increases aggregate bandwidth and provides link redundancy
Provides automatic failover to a backup switch path
explanation: EtherChannel/link aggregation bundles multiple physical links into one logical link, increasing aggregate bandwidth and providing redundancy if a member link fails - while STP still treats the bundle as a single link.

question: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is configured on a switch to form an EtherChannel with a neighbor. Which LACP mode causes the port to actively send LACP PDUs to initiate negotiation?
explanation: LACP active mode actively sends LACPDUs to negotiate the bundle; passive only responds. 'On' forces a channel without LACP negotiation, and 'auto'/'desirable' are PAgP (Cisco), not LACP.

question: An organization replaces their routers with Layer 3 switches to reduce inter-VLAN routing latency. What capability distinguishes a Layer 3 switch from a Layer 2 switch?
choices: Layer 3 switches eliminate STP from the network
Layer 3 switches can route IP traffic between VLANs using SVIs or routed ports
explanation: A Layer 3 switch can route IP between VLANs in hardware using switched virtual interfaces (SVIs) or routed ports, eliminating the router-on-a-stick bottleneck. 802.1Q trunking and PoE are also available on Layer 2 switches.

question: A network engineer assigns a loopback address to a router. Why is a loopback interface preferred over a physical interface for router ID assignment and management access?
choices: A loopback interface is always up regardless of physical link status
A loopback interface supports higher throughput than physical interfaces
A loopback interface provides PoE capability
A loopback interface operates at Layer 2
explanation: A loopback interface is virtual and always up regardless of physical port status, making it ideal for a stable router ID and management/management-plane reachability. It has no bearing on throughput, PoE, or Layer 2.

question: A single router port is divided into multiple logical subinterfaces, each with 802.1Q encapsulation for a different VLAN. What is this configuration called?
explanation: Dividing one router/L3 interface into 802.1Q-tagged subinterfaces, one per VLAN, to route between VLANs over a single trunk is called router-on-a-stick. EtherChannel bundles links, VTP propagates VLAN databases, and inter-VLAN bridging is not a standard term.

question: An NTP hierarchy is used to synchronize clocks across a network. What is an NTP Stratum 0 device?
choices: A router that receives time from an ISP
A highly accurate reference clock such as a GPS or atomic clock
A switch that distributes time to end devices
A client that syncs from a Stratum 1 server
explanation: Stratum 0 is the reference time source itself - a GPS receiver, atomic clock, or radio clock - that is not on the network. Stratum 1 servers attach directly to it; higher strata sync downstream.

question: NTP servers are organized in a stratum hierarchy. Which stratum level describes a server that is directly connected to a Stratum 0 time source?
explanation: A Stratum 1 server is directly connected to a Stratum 0 reference clock and is the most authoritative network NTP source. Each step away from Stratum 0 increments the stratum number.

question: A syslog message is received with a severity level of 3. According to the standard syslog severity scale, what does severity 3 indicate?
explanation: On the syslog 0-7 scale, severity 3 is Error. 0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notice, 6 Informational, 7 Debug.

question: An SNMP manager needs reliable delivery of alert notifications from a managed device, with confirmation that the notification was received. Which SNMP notification type satisfies this requirement?
explanation: An SNMP inform is an acknowledged notification - the receiver confirms receipt, so the sender can retransmit if needed. A trap is fire-and-forget (unacknowledged); get/set are polling/configuration operations.

question: NetFlow is configured on border routers. Which component receives the exported flow records and provides analysis, reporting, and storage?
explanation: The NetFlow collector receives exported flow records from exporters (routers/switches) and stores, aggregates, and reports on them. The exporter generates the records; a probe captures traffic; RMON is a different SNMP-based technology.

question: A network operations team is creating procedures that define who must be notified and in what order when an outage exceeds a defined threshold. What type of procedure is this?
explanation: A procedure defining who is notified, in what order, and at what thresholds during an incident is an escalation procedure. Change management governs changes, while DR/BC plans address recovery and continuity more broadly.

question: A network team schedules all non-emergency changes to occur between 2:00 AM and 4:00 AM on weekends. What is the primary purpose of this practice?
choices: To minimize business disruption during periods of low network usage
To avoid NTP synchronization issues during business hours
To allow new firmware to download without impacting QoS
To comply with change management audit schedules
explanation: Scheduling changes during a low-usage maintenance window (e.g., 2-4 AM weekends) minimizes business disruption and user impact if something goes wrong. The other reasons are not the primary driver.

question: An auditor asks the network team for a complete list of all switches, routers, firewalls, and their software versions. Which IT management practice produces and maintains this information?
explanation: Maintaining an authoritative inventory of all devices and their software/firmware versions is asset management (often via a CMDB). Baselining, capacity planning, and change management use that data but do not produce the inventory.

question: An organization places its web servers, email gateway, and public DNS servers in a network segment that is accessible from the internet but isolated from the internal corporate LAN. What is this network segment called?
explanation: A DMZ (demilitarized zone) is a screened segment hosting internet-facing services (web, email, public DNS) while isolating them from the trusted internal LAN, so a compromised public server cannot directly reach internal hosts.

question: A firewall administrator adds specific permit rules to an ACL. A packet that does not match any rule reaches the end of the ACL. What happens by default?
choices: The packet is forwarded to the next hop
The packet is sent to the SIEM for analysis
The packet is queued for manual review
The packet is dropped by the implicit deny-all rule
explanation: ACLs end with an implicit deny-all: any packet matching no explicit permit is silently dropped. It is not forwarded, queued, or auto-sent to a SIEM by the ACL itself.

question: A security team deploys a system on the network that appears to be a vulnerable server but actually monitors and logs any interaction with it. What is this system called?
explanation: A honeypot is a decoy system that looks vulnerable but exists to attract, monitor, and log attacker activity for detection and study. SIEM aggregates logs, IPS blocks attacks, and a scanner finds vulnerabilities.

question: A healthcare company needs to prevent employees from emailing patient records or uploading them to personal cloud storage. Which security technology monitors and blocks unauthorized data transfers?
explanation: Data Loss Prevention (DLP) inspects content in email, web uploads, and endpoints to detect and block unauthorized transfer of sensitive data such as patient records. IDS/firewall/SIEM do not enforce content-based data egress policy.

question: Employees receive emails that appear to come from their company's IT department, asking them to click a link and enter their credentials to 'verify their account.' What type of attack is this?
explanation: A mass email impersonating IT and luring users to a credential-harvesting link is phishing. Vishing is voice, smishing is SMS, and whaling specifically targets high-value executives.

question: A database administrator with legitimate access to customer data copies thousands of records to a personal USB drive and removes them from the building. Which threat category does this represent?
explanation: An authorized user abusing legitimate access to exfiltrate data is an insider threat. It is not an external attacker, an APT campaign, or a zero-day exploit.

question: An organization conducts a formal review to determine whether network configurations, access controls, and security procedures comply with policy and regulatory requirements. What type of assessment is this?
explanation: A formal compliance review of configurations, access controls, and procedures against policy/regulation is a security audit. A pen test actively exploits, a vulnerability scan enumerates weaknesses, and a risk assessment evaluates likelihood/impact.

question: A technician wants to continuously ping a remote host to monitor for intermittent packet loss over an extended period. Which ping option (on Windows) sends continuous pings without stopping?
explanation: On Windows, ping -t pings continuously until interrupted (Ctrl+C), ideal for watching intermittent loss over time. -n sets a fixed count, -l sets packet size, and -a resolves the hostname.

question: A technician needs to identify where in the network path packets are being delayed or dropped between a workstation and a remote server. Which command reveals each hop along the route and its latency?
explanation: tracert (Windows) / traceroute (Unix) sends TTL-incrementing probes to reveal each hop along the path and its latency, isolating where delay or loss occurs. nslookup resolves DNS, netstat shows local sockets, and arp -a shows the ARP cache.

question: A technician suspects a service is not listening on the expected port after a restart. Which command shows currently active TCP/UDP connections and listening ports on a local machine?
explanation: netstat -an lists all active TCP/UDP connections and listening sockets with numeric addresses/ports, confirming whether a service is bound and listening. ipconfig, arp, and tracert do not show listening ports.

question: Which TWO statements correctly describe OSPF as a routing protocol? (Select 2)
choices: OSPF uses Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to calculate loop-free shortest paths
OSPF has a maximum hop count of 15, limiting it to small networks
OSPF organizes routers into areas to limit LSA flooding
OSPF is a distance-vector protocol that uses hop count as its metric
explanation: OSPF runs Dijkstra's SPF for loop-free shortest paths and uses areas to limit LSA flooding and SPF scope. It is link-state (not distance-vector) and has no 15-hop limit (that is RIP).

question: Which TWO benefits does VLAN implementation provide on a switched network? (Select 2)
choices: Eliminates the need for routing between network segments
Reduces the size of broadcast domains, limiting unnecessary traffic
Automatically encrypts traffic between VLAN members
Provides logical segmentation that improves security between groups
explanation: VLANs shrink broadcast domains (less unnecessary flooding) and provide logical segmentation that improves security between groups. They do NOT remove the need for routing between segments or encrypt traffic.

question: A Windows network technician is troubleshooting connectivity from a workstation to a remote server. Which TWO built-in Windows commands are most directly useful for path analysis and port/connection verification? (Select 2)
explanation: tracert performs hop-by-hop path analysis and netstat verifies local connections/listening ports - the two most directly useful here. nslookup tests DNS and ipconfig shows local IP config, which are secondary to path/port issues.

question: Match each STP port state to its correct description.
itemsRight: Actively forwards user frames and populates MAC table
Discards user frames; receives BPDUs only to prevent loops
Receives and sends BPDUs; no MAC learning; no forwarding
Populates MAC address table but does not forward user frames
explanation: STP states: Blocking discards user frames and only listens to BPDUs; Listening sends/receives BPDUs with no MAC learning or forwarding; Learning builds the MAC table but still does not forward; Forwarding passes user frames and learns MACs.

question: Match each network monitoring technology to its primary function.
itemsRight: Device-generated event and error message collection
Device metric polling via OIDs and MIBs
IP traffic flow export and analysis
Remote traffic statistics via embedded hardware agents
explanation: NetFlow exports IP flow records for traffic analysis, SNMP polls device metrics via OIDs/MIBs, Syslog collects device-generated event/error messages, and RMON gathers remote traffic statistics via embedded agents.

question: Arrange the CompTIA Network+ troubleshooting methodology steps in the correct order.
items: Implement the solution or escalate
Identify the problem
Test the theory to determine the cause
Establish a theory of probable cause
Verify full system functionality and document findings
explanation: CompTIA's methodology: (1) identify the problem, (2) establish a theory of probable cause, (3) test the theory, (4) establish a plan and implement the solution or escalate, (5) verify full functionality and document.

question: Arrange the following security incident response phases in the correct order (NIST/PICERL framework).
items: Containment — isolate affected systems to prevent further spread
Identification — detect and confirm that a security incident has occurred
Eradication — remove the root cause and any malicious artifacts
Recovery — restore systems and verify normal operations
Lessons learned — document findings and improve defenses
explanation: The PICERL/NIST flow is Preparation then Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, and Lessons Learned - detect/confirm, isolate, remove the cause, restore and verify, then document and improve.

question: Evaluate each statement about routing protocols and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: OSPF is a link-state protocol that uses Dijkstra's shortest path first algorithm.
RIP supports a maximum of 30 hops, making it suitable for large enterprise networks.
BGP is the exterior gateway protocol used to exchange routing information between autonomous systems on the internet.
EIGRP is often described as a hybrid routing protocol because it combines characteristics of both distance-vector and link-state protocols.
explanation: True: OSPF is link-state using Dijkstra SPF, BGP is the internet's exterior gateway protocol between ASes, and EIGRP is a hybrid (advanced distance-vector with link-state traits). False: RIP's limit is 15 hops, so it is not suited to large networks.

question: Evaluate each statement about VLANs and 802.1Q trunking and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: VLANs logically segment a switched network at Layer 2, reducing broadcast domain size.
IEEE 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame header to identify VLAN membership.
The native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk is permanently fixed to VLAN 1 and cannot be changed.
Frames belonging to the native VLAN are transmitted untagged across an 802.1Q trunk link.
explanation: True: VLANs segment Layer 2 and shrink broadcast domains, 802.1Q adds a 4-byte tag, and native-VLAN frames cross the trunk untagged. False: the native VLAN defaults to VLAN 1 but is configurable.

question: Evaluate each statement about DMZ design and network security and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: A DMZ is a network segment between an untrusted external network and the trusted internal LAN.
Servers placed in a DMZ should have unrestricted access to internal LAN resources for seamless operation.
Web servers, email gateways, and public DNS servers are commonly placed in a DMZ.
A single firewall with only two interfaces (one external, one internal) is sufficient to create a true three-zone DMZ.
explanation: True: a DMZ sits between the untrusted outside and trusted inside and hosts public-facing servers. False: DMZ hosts should have tightly restricted (not unrestricted) access to the internal LAN, and a true screened-subnet DMZ needs two firewalls or a three-legged firewall, not a simple two-interface firewall.

question: A workstation boots and sends a broadcast frame to find a DHCP server. Which message initiates the DHCP DORA process?
explanation: DHCP DORA begins with the client broadcasting a DHCPDISCOVER to locate any DHCP server. OFFER, REQUEST, and ACK are the subsequent steps.

question: After receiving a DHCPDISCOVER, a DHCP server responds with an available IP address, subnet mask, lease time, and gateway. What is this response message called?
explanation: After DISCOVER, a server replies with a DHCPOFFER proposing an address, mask, lease time, and gateway. ACK confirms later, REQUEST is the client's acceptance, and NAK declines.

question: A DHCP client receives offers from two servers and must formally accept one. Which broadcast message does the client send to accept the chosen offer?
explanation: The client broadcasts a DHCPREQUEST to formally accept one server's offer (broadcast so other servers know their offers were declined). DISCOVER starts the process and OFFER/ACK come from the server.

question: The DHCP server receives the client's DHCPREQUEST and finalizes the address assignment. Which message confirms the lease to the client?
explanation: The server finalizes the lease with a DHCPACK, confirming the bound address and parameters to the client. A DHCPNAK would instead reject the request.

question: A host needs the MAC address of another device on the same subnet. It sends an ARP request. How is the ARP request transmitted on the local network?
choices: Broadcast to all devices on the subnet
Multicast to the ARP multicast group
explanation: An ARP request is broadcast (destination MAC ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff) so every host on the subnet sees it; only the owner of the target IP replies via unicast. ARP has no anycast or multicast group.

question: A host sends an ARP message announcing its own IP-to-MAC binding to update neighbor ARP caches after a NIC replacement. What is this called?
explanation: A gratuitous ARP is an unsolicited ARP announcing a host's own IP-to-MAC binding, used to refresh neighbor caches after a NIC/IP change or for failover. Proxy ARP answers for another host, and ARP poisoning is malicious.

question: In IPv6 SLAAC, a host automatically configures its own global unicast address. Which ICMPv6 message does the host listen for to obtain the network prefix and default gateway?
explanation: In SLAAC the host listens for ICMPv6 Router Advertisements (often after sending a Router Solicitation) to learn the on-link prefix and default gateway, then forms its own address. Neighbor Solicitation is for address resolution/DAD.

question: A host using SLAAC generates its IPv6 interface identifier from its 48-bit MAC address by inserting FF:FE in the middle and flipping the 7th bit. What is this process called?
explanation: EUI-64 derives the 64-bit IPv6 interface ID from a 48-bit MAC by inserting FFFE in the middle and flipping the universal/local (7th) bit. DAD checks uniqueness and privacy extensions randomize the ID instead.

question: A client's stub resolver sends a DNS query to its configured DNS server and expects a complete answer. The DNS server queries other servers on the client's behalf. What type of query did the client send?
explanation: A recursive query asks the configured DNS server to do all the work and return a final answer; the server then performs iterative queries on the client's behalf.

question: A DNS server receives a query it cannot answer authoritatively. Instead of resolving it fully, it returns a referral to another DNS server. What type of query response is this?
explanation: An iterative response is a referral: the server returns the best information it has (e.g., the next server to ask) rather than fully resolving the name. Recursive resolution would instead return the final answer.

question: Modern switched networks operate in full-duplex mode. What is the primary advantage of full-duplex over half-duplex Ethernet?
choices: Full-duplex doubles the collision domain size
Full-duplex allows simultaneous transmission and reception, eliminating collisions
Full-duplex reduces the number of required switch ports
Full-duplex only works with fiber cabling
explanation: Full-duplex lets a station send and receive simultaneously over dedicated switch links, eliminating collisions entirely (CSMA/CD is unnecessary). It does not depend on fiber or reduce port counts.

question: CSMA/CD is the media access method used by which network technology to detect and recover from collisions on a shared medium?
explanation: CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is used by traditional half-duplex Ethernet on a shared medium to detect collisions and back off. Wi-Fi uses CSMA/CA, and Token Ring uses token passing.

question: A network technician is verifying cable compatibility for a 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet) installation. Which is the minimum cable category required to support 1 Gbps at 100 meters?
explanation: Cat5e is the minimum category rated for 1000BASE-T (1 Gbps) over the full 100 m. Cat6/6A/7 also work but exceed the minimum requirement.

question: A data center requires 10 Gbps connections to server racks over runs up to 100 meters. Which cable category is specifically rated for 10GBASE-T at that full distance?
explanation: Cat6A is specifically rated for 10GBASE-T at the full 100 m. Plain Cat6 only reaches ~55 m at 10G, and Cat5e is not 10G-rated.

question: A data center technician is connecting SFP+ transceivers to fiber patch panels. Which small form-factor fiber connector uses a latch mechanism and is the most commonly deployed connector in modern data centers?
explanation: The LC (Lucent Connector) is a small-form-factor latching connector and the most common choice for SFP/SFP+ in modern high-density data centers. SC is larger, ST is legacy bayonet, and MPO is for multi-fiber trunks.

question: A telecom technician terminates a fiber run to a patch panel using a connector with a square-shaped ferrule and a push-pull coupling mechanism. Which connector type is this?
explanation: The SC connector has a square body and a push-pull (snap) coupling, common in telecom and FTTH. LC is smaller, ST is a round bayonet, and MPO carries many fibers.

question: An older campus network uses fiber patch cables with a round connector and a twist-lock bayonet coupling. Which fiber connector type is this?
explanation: The ST connector is round with a twist-lock bayonet coupling, common in older multimode plants. SC/LC are push-pull and FC is threaded.

question: A data center engineer is installing a high-density fiber backbone between MDF and IDF panels using 12-fiber trunk cables. Which connector type terminates these multi-fiber trunk assemblies?
explanation: MPO/MTP connectors terminate multi-fiber (e.g., 12-fiber) trunk assemblies for high-density backbones between MDF/IDF. LC/SC duplex carry only two fibers and ST one.

question: A broadband technician installs coaxial cable from a distribution amplifier to a home for internet service. Which coaxial cable type is standard for DOCSIS cable internet and satellite TV installations?
explanation: RG-6 is the standard 75-ohm coax for DOCSIS cable internet and satellite TV. RG-59 is thinner legacy CCTV/video, while RG-58/RG-8 are 50-ohm cables used for older Ethernet/radio.

question: A network engineer is configuring WPA2-Personal on access points. Which encryption protocol does WPA2 use that replaced the vulnerable TKIP used in WPA?
explanation: WPA2 uses AES-CCMP for confidentiality and integrity, replacing the RC4-based TKIP used by WPA. WEP and RC4 are the older, broken schemes.

question: A wireless administrator is decommissioning older access points that only supported WPA with TKIP. Why was TKIP replaced?
choices: TKIP was found to be cryptographically weak and vulnerable to attacks including the KRACK vulnerability
TKIP required dedicated hardware accelerators unavailable in older APs
TKIP increased overhead and reduced throughput below 1 Mbps
explanation: TKIP was retired because it is built on the weak RC4 cipher with a flawed Michael MIC, leaving it vulnerable to key-recovery and injection attacks. It was a stopgap for WPA hardware and is no longer considered secure.

question: WPA3-Personal introduces a new handshake to replace the four-way handshake used in WPA2. What specific threat does this WPA3 improvement protect against?
choices: Offline dictionary attacks against the pre-shared key
explanation: WPA3-Personal's SAE handshake replaces the WPA2 four-way handshake and resists offline dictionary/brute-force attacks against the PSK (and adds forward secrecy). WPS PIN and deauth attacks are mitigated by other controls.

question: A network engineer accesses routers via a dedicated management network that is physically separate from the production data network. What type of management access is this?
explanation: Managing devices over a dedicated, physically separate network is out-of-band management, so administrators retain access even if the production network is down. In-band uses the production data path.

question: An administrator SSHs into a router using the same network interfaces that carry production user traffic. What type of management access describes this?
explanation: Reaching a device over the same interfaces that carry production traffic is in-band management - simple but unavailable if the data network fails. Out-of-band uses a separate path/console.

question: A security team requires that all SSH sessions to production servers must pass through a hardened, audited intermediate server that logs all commands. What is this intermediary called?
explanation: A jump server (bastion host) is a hardened, audited intermediary that all administrative sessions must traverse, centralizing access control and command logging. Proxies and load balancers serve traffic-handling roles, not admin auditing.

question: VoIP call quality is degraded even though the average one-way delay is acceptable. Users report choppy audio. Which metric specifically measures the inconsistency in packet arrival times that causes this symptom?
explanation: Jitter is the variation in packet inter-arrival times; high jitter causes choppy VoIP even when average latency is fine, because the jitter buffer under/overruns. Latency is the delay itself and packet loss is dropped packets.

question: A network engineer measures the time it takes for a single packet to travel from a source host to a destination host. Which network performance metric is being measured?
explanation: Latency is the time for a single packet to travel from source to destination (one-way or round-trip). Throughput/bandwidth measure data rate and jitter measures delay variation.

question: A WAN link is rated at 100 Mbps, but a technician running iPerf measures only 60 Mbps of usable data transfer. Which term describes the 60 Mbps actual measured rate?
explanation: Throughput is the actual measured data transfer rate achieved (60 Mbps), as opposed to the link's rated bandwidth/capacity (100 Mbps). Overhead, congestion, and protocol behavior reduce throughput below bandwidth.

question: A network engineer configures DSCP markings on switches and routers to ensure that voice traffic receives lower latency and video traffic gets guaranteed bandwidth. What technology enables this traffic prioritization?
explanation: Quality of Service (QoS) classifies and prioritizes traffic (e.g., via DSCP) so latency-sensitive voice and bandwidth-sensitive video get appropriate treatment. VLANs segment, NAT translates, and shaping is just one QoS tool.

question: A VoIP administrator notices call quality degrades when the WAN link exceeds 80% utilization. Users report audio drop-outs. Which network condition is most directly causing this VoIP quality issue?
choices: Excessive packet loss and queuing delay under congestion
explanation: When a WAN link saturates (>~80%), queues fill and packets are delayed or dropped; VoIP is highly sensitive to this packet loss and queuing delay, causing drop-outs. DNS/DHCP/subnet issues would not correlate with link utilization.

question: A security architect needs to select an encryption algorithm for encrypting large volumes of data at rest. Which algorithm is a symmetric block cipher standardized by NIST, supporting 128-, 192-, and 256-bit key lengths?
explanation: AES is the NIST-standardized symmetric block cipher supporting 128/192/256-bit keys, ideal for fast bulk encryption of data at rest. RSA/ECC/Diffie-Hellman are asymmetric algorithms.

question: A TLS session setup requires securely exchanging encryption keys between a client and server that have never communicated before. Which algorithm is commonly used for this asymmetric key exchange?
explanation: RSA is widely used for asymmetric key exchange and to protect the symmetric session key during TLS setup between parties with no prior shared secret. AES/3DES are symmetric and MD5 is a hash.

question: A developer must choose between symmetric and asymmetric encryption for encrypting large files efficiently. Which statement correctly explains why symmetric encryption is preferred for bulk data?
choices: Symmetric encryption is significantly faster because it uses the same key for both encryption and decryption
Symmetric encryption uses a public/private key pair which reduces processing overhead
Asymmetric encryption is faster but requires more storage space
Symmetric encryption does not require a shared secret between parties
explanation: Symmetric encryption uses one shared key for both operations and is far faster/less CPU-intensive than asymmetric, making it the choice for bulk data; asymmetric is reserved for key exchange and signatures.

question: In a PKI, which entity is responsible for issuing, signing, and revoking digital certificates that bind a public key to an identity?
explanation: The Certificate Authority (CA) issues, signs, and revokes certificates that bind a public key to a verified identity. The RA handles vetting/enrollment, while CRL/OCSP publish revocation status.

question: A developer creates an SSL certificate for an internal test server without using a trusted CA. When users browse to the site, they receive a certificate warning. What type of certificate was used?
explanation: A self-signed certificate is signed by its own key rather than a trusted CA, so browsers cannot build a trust chain and warn the user. Wildcard/EV/SAN describe scope/validation, not the trust problem.

question: A company hosts multiple subdomains (shop.example.com, mail.example.com, api.example.com) and wants a single certificate to secure all of them. Which certificate type uses the *.example.com format?
explanation: A wildcard certificate (*.example.com) secures all single-level subdomains of one domain. SAN lists explicit multiple names, EV is high-assurance validation, and self-signed is untrusted.

question: A company needs a single certificate that covers example.com, api.example.com, and example.net — three distinct names that a wildcard cannot cover. Which certificate type lists multiple FQDNs in a single certificate?
explanation: A SAN (Subject Alternative Name) certificate lists multiple specific FQDNs - even across different domains - in one certificate, which a single-domain wildcard cannot do.

question: A technician pings a remote server and receives replies, but the round-trip time is 350 ms and varies between 280 and 420 ms. There is no packet loss. What network condition is most likely indicated?
choices: Cable fault causing intermittent signal loss
Network congestion or long path causing high latency and jitter
Duplex mismatch on local switch port
explanation: Successful replies with high, highly variable RTT and no loss indicate latency and jitter from congestion or a long path, not DNS, cabling, or duplex faults (which usually cause loss or failures).

question: A wireless client associates with the access point and receives a valid IP address via DHCP, but cannot reach any internet resources. Pinging the default gateway succeeds. What should be investigated next?
choices: Routing or NAT configuration beyond the gateway
explanation: Reaching the gateway but nothing beyond it points to a problem past the gateway - routing or NAT/upstream connectivity - rather than DHCP, RF, or 802.1X (which would have prevented the IP/gateway success).

question: A technician suspects a stale ARP entry is causing a connectivity issue after a server's NIC was replaced. Which command displays the current ARP cache on a Windows or Linux workstation?
explanation: arp -a displays the local ARP cache on Windows and Linux, letting you spot a stale IP-to-MAC entry after a NIC swap. netstat shows sockets, nslookup DNS, and ipconfig the IP configuration.

question: The DHCP DORA process involves four messages. Which TWO messages are sent as broadcasts by the DHCP client? (Select 2)
explanation: The client broadcasts DHCPDISCOVER (no IP yet) and DHCPREQUEST (so non-selected servers see the decline). DHCPOFFER and DHCPACK are server replies.

question: A data center engineer is selecting fiber connectors for a high-density backbone installation. Which TWO connector types are most commonly used in high-density data center fiber deployments? (Select 2)
explanation: High-density data center fiber predominantly uses LC (small-form-factor duplex) and MPO/MTP (multi-fiber trunk) connectors. ST and SC are older/larger and less common in dense racks.

question: Which TWO statements correctly describe asymmetric encryption? (Select 2)
choices: Uses a single shared key for both encryption and decryption
Uses a mathematically related public and private key pair
Is generally slower than symmetric encryption for bulk data encryption
Is the preferred algorithm for encrypting large files due to speed
explanation: Asymmetric encryption uses a mathematically related public/private key pair and is comparatively slow, so it is used for key exchange and signatures rather than bulk data (which symmetric handles).

question: Match each DHCP message to its correct description.
itemsRight: Server finalizes the IP lease and confirms assignment to the client
Client broadcasts to locate available DHCP servers
Server proposes an available IP address and configuration to the client
Client broadcasts to formally accept the offered IP address
explanation: DHCPDISCOVER: client broadcasts to find servers. DHCPOFFER: server proposes an address/config. DHCPREQUEST: client broadcasts acceptance of one offer. DHCPACK: server confirms and finalizes the lease.

question: Match each fiber optic connector type to its primary characteristic.
itemsRight: Bayonet-style twist-lock coupling; common in older multimode installations
Multi-fiber push-on connector used for high-density trunk cables
Push-pull square-body connector common in telecom and FTTH
Small form-factor latch connector widely deployed in modern data centers
explanation: LC is the small latch connector dominant in modern data centers, SC is the square push-pull common in telecom/FTTH, ST is the legacy bayonet twist-lock for older multimode, and MPO/MTP terminates high-density multi-fiber trunks.

question: Arrange the four DHCP messages in the correct order of the DORA process.
items: DHCPACK
DHCPDISCOVER
DHCPREQUEST
DHCPOFFER
explanation: The DHCP lease handshake order is DORA: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge.

question: A workstation has LAN connectivity but cannot reach the internet. Arrange these troubleshooting steps in the most logical order.
items: Verify the default gateway is reachable using ping
Confirm IP address, subnet mask, and gateway with ipconfig/ifconfig
Test DNS resolution using nslookup or dig
Ping a public IP address (e.g., 8.8.8.8) to test internet routing
Ping a remote hostname to confirm end-to-end DNS and routing
explanation: Logical outbound troubleshooting: verify local IP/mask/gateway, ping the gateway, ping a public IP (8.8.8.8) to test routing, test DNS with nslookup/dig, then ping a hostname to confirm end-to-end name resolution and routing.

question: Evaluate each statement about ARP and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: ARP resolves Layer 3 IP addresses to Layer 2 MAC addresses on a local network.
ARP requests are sent as Ethernet broadcast frames to all devices on the subnet.
ARP replies are also sent as broadcast frames so all hosts update their ARP caches.
ARP cache poisoning exploits ARP's lack of authentication to redirect traffic in a MITM attack.
explanation: True: ARP maps IP to MAC locally, requests are broadcast, and unauthenticated ARP enables cache-poisoning MITM. False: ARP replies are unicast back to the requester, not broadcast.

question: Evaluate each statement about Ethernet cabling standards and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: Cat6A supports 10 Gbps (10GBASE-T) at distances up to 100 meters.
Cat5e supports 10 Gbps for runs up to 55 meters in low-crosstalk environments.
Plenum-rated (CMP) cables are required in air-handling spaces per fire safety codes.
A straight-through cable is used to connect a workstation to a switch port.
explanation: True: Cat6A does 10G at 100 m, plenum cable is code-required in air-handling spaces, and a straight-through cable connects a PC to a switch. False: Cat5e is not rated for 10G (that 55 m figure applies to Cat6).

question: Evaluate each statement about encryption and indicate whether it is True or False.
statements: Symmetric encryption uses a mathematically related public and private key pair.
AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is a widely used symmetric block cipher.
RSA is an asymmetric algorithm commonly used for key exchange and digital signatures.
Asymmetric encryption is faster than symmetric encryption for encrypting large volumes of data.
explanation: True: AES is a symmetric block cipher and RSA is asymmetric (key exchange/signatures). False: symmetric uses a single shared key (not a public/private pair), and asymmetric is slower than symmetric for bulk data.

question: In SNMP, each manageable resource on a device (such as interface bandwidth or CPU utilization) is identified by a unique numerical identifier in a hierarchical tree. What is this identifier called?
explanation: An OID (Object Identifier) is the unique dotted-decimal identifier locating a managed object in the SNMP MIB tree. The community string is an access password, and the MIB is the overall definition catalog.

question: An SNMP manager queries a switch for interface statistics. The switch references an internal database that defines the structure and meaning of all queryable objects. What is this database called?
explanation: The MIB (Management Information Base) is the hierarchical database defining the structure and meaning of every queryable object on an SNMP agent; OIDs are the addresses of objects within it. The community string is an access password and a trap receiver collects notifications.

question: A host is assigned the IPv6 address 2001:db8::1/64. Which address type does the prefix 2000::/3 identify?
explanation: The 2000::/3 block is IPv6 global unicast - globally routable addresses, of which 2001:db8::/32 is the documentation range. Link-local is fe80::/10, ULA fc00::/7, and multicast ff00::/8.

question: An enterprise uses IPv6 addresses starting with fd00:: for internal routing that should not be routed on the public internet. Which IPv6 address type does the fc00::/7 prefix represent?
explanation: fc00::/7 (commonly fd00::/8 in practice) is Unique Local Address space - private IPv6 not routed on the public internet, analogous to RFC 1918. Global unicast is 2000::/3 and link-local fe80::/10.

question: An IPv6 router sends Neighbor Discovery messages to all routers on the segment. These messages are addressed to the all-routers multicast group. Which prefix identifies all IPv6 multicast addresses?
explanation: All IPv6 multicast addresses fall under ff00::/8 (e.g., ff02::2 = all-routers). fe80::/10 is link-local, fc00::/7 is ULA, and 2000::/3 is global unicast.

question: What is the IPv6 loopback address, equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4?
explanation: ::1 is the IPv6 loopback address, equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4. fe80::1 would be link-local and ::ffff:127.0.0.1 is an IPv4-mapped form.

question: A cloud provider needs to extend Layer 2 network segments across a Layer 3 data center fabric to support VM migration between racks. Which overlay protocol encapsulates Layer 2 Ethernet frames in UDP packets for this purpose?
explanation: VXLAN encapsulates Layer 2 Ethernet frames in UDP (VNI-tagged) to stretch L2 segments across an L3 fabric, enabling VM mobility between racks. GRE/L2TP are generic tunnels and MPLS is label switching.

question: A network engineer needs to tunnel non-IP protocols (such as IPX) or multicast traffic across an IP-only WAN that does not support these natively. Which tunneling protocol encapsulates any Layer 3 protocol inside IP packets?
explanation: GRE encapsulates virtually any Layer 3 protocol (including non-IP and multicast) inside IP, letting it traverse an IP-only WAN. It provides no encryption (pair with IPSec for that).

question: A service provider uses a technology where routers attach short fixed-length labels to packets at the network edge and forward them based on labels rather than IP lookups, improving WAN routing speed. What is this technology?
explanation: MPLS attaches short fixed-length labels at the network edge and forwards on label swapping instead of per-hop IP lookups, speeding provider WAN forwarding and enabling traffic engineering/VPNs.

question: An organization is replacing its fixed MPLS WAN with a solution that uses broadband, LTE, and MPLS simultaneously, selecting the best path per application based on real-time link quality. Which WAN technology provides this capability?
explanation: SD-WAN abstracts multiple transports (MPLS, broadband, LTE) and steers traffic per-application using real-time link metrics and centralized policy, replacing rigid single-link MPLS designs.

question: A data center builds virtual tenant networks on top of shared physical infrastructure, with each tenant's traffic logically separated but running over the same underlying switches and routers. What term describes this virtual network built on top of a physical network?
explanation: A virtual network built logically on top of shared physical infrastructure is an overlay (e.g., VXLAN); the physical transport beneath it is the underlay.

question: A remote worker's VPN client is configured so that only traffic destined for the corporate network goes through the VPN tunnel, while all other internet traffic flows directly through the local ISP connection. What is this configuration called?
explanation: Split tunneling sends only corporate-bound traffic through the VPN while other internet traffic uses the local ISP path directly, reducing VPN load at some security cost. Full tunnel sends everything through the VPN.

question: A large enterprise has hundreds of access points deployed campus-wide. All APs receive their configuration, firmware, and policy from a central device, eliminating the need to individually configure each AP. Which AP deployment model is this?
explanation: Controller-based (thin/lightweight) APs pull configuration, firmware, RF, and policy centrally from a WLC, so APs are not configured individually. Autonomous (fat) APs are managed one by one.

question: Lightweight access points communicate with a wireless LAN controller using a tunneling protocol that carries both control and data traffic. Which protocol is the IETF standard for this WLC-AP communication?
explanation: CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points) is the IETF-standard tunneling protocol carrying control and (optionally) data between lightweight APs and the WLC. LWAPP is its Cisco predecessor.

question: A wireless LAN controller (WLC) is deployed in a large enterprise network. Which THREE primary functions does the WLC perform?
choices: Provides internet routing for all wireless clients
Centrally manages AP configuration, firmware, and RF settings
Handles authentication and applies wireless security policies
Aggregates roaming decisions to maintain client sessions across APs
explanation: A WLC's core function is centralized management of AP configuration, firmware, and RF (channel/power) settings; it also handles security policy and roaming. It does not provide internet routing for clients.

question: A warehouse needs wireless coverage in all directions throughout a large open floor. Which antenna type radiates RF energy equally in all horizontal directions, making it ideal for central placement?
explanation: An omnidirectional antenna radiates roughly equally in all horizontal directions (doughnut pattern), ideal for central placement to cover an open floor. Yagi/parabolic/panel antennas are directional.

question: A building-to-building wireless link requires maximum gain focused in a single direction to bridge two structures 500 meters apart. Which antenna type provides high gain in one narrow direction?
explanation: A Yagi is a directional antenna delivering high gain in one narrow beam, suited to point-to-point building bridges. Omnidirectional/dipole/rubber-duck antennas spread energy too widely for a focused 500 m link.

question: An 802.11n access point achieves higher throughput by combining two adjacent 20 MHz channels into a single wider channel. What is this technique called?
explanation: Channel bonding combines two adjacent 20 MHz channels into a 40 MHz channel (introduced with 802.11n) to roughly double throughput. MIMO/spatial multiplexing and OFDMA are separate techniques.

question: An 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) access point supports multiple channel widths. Which channel widths does 802.11ac support on the 5 GHz band? (Select the most complete correct list)
